coredata.dc:title: Fin geometry optimization for enhanced PCM solidification in solar cooking thermal storage system: Numerical simulation and experimental validation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kiros, Abraha Kahsay
Zeru, Balewgize Amare
Desisa, Debela Geneti
Tewolde, Desta Goytom
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the enhancement of heat transfer in phase change materials (PCMs) for solar cooking applications by optimizing fin dimensions to address the low thermal conductivity of PCM during the discharging process. A numerical and experimental analysis was conducted to evaluate the impact of fin length and thickness on solidification time and energy storage capacity, balancing the trade-off between heat transfer improvement and PCM volume reduction. Using ANSYS 16.0 for computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations and response surface methodology (RSM) for design optimization, the study employed solar salt (53 % KNO3, 6 % NaNO3, 41 % NaNO2) with a melting point of 142 °C and latent heat of 110 kJ/kg. Key parameters included fin lengths (70 - 140 mm) and thicknesses (0.8 - 1.5 mm), validated experimentally. Results demonstrated that increasing fin length significantly outperformed thickness enhancement; a fin with 1.5 mm thickness and 140 mm length reduced solidification time by 65.97 % compared to a finless system. RSM optimization identified a fin configuration of 0.8 mm thickness and 140 mm length as optimal, achieving complete solidification in 10.21 hours while releasing 2237.91 kJ of stored energy. These findings highlight the critical role of fin geometry in improving PCM efficiency, enabling effective solar energy storage for extended use, and advancing sustainable alternatives to conventional cooking fuels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase change materials
Response surface methodology
Solar salt
Optimization
CFD

Effect of fins on pan surface temperature distribution
Response surface methodology (RSM) for geometric optimization

Solidification rate and heat storage results of the fin design points.

head.ce:title: Fin geometry optimization for enhanced PCM solidification in solar cooking thermal storage system: Numerical simulation and experimental validation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Mechanical Engineering, Jimma University, Jimma, 378, Oromia, Ethiopia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aFaculty of Mechanical Engineering, Jimma University, Jimma, 378, Oromia, Ethiopia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Raya University, Maichew, 92, Tigray, Ethiopia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, Raya University, Maichew, 92, Tigray, Ethiopia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the enhancement of heat transfer in phase change materials (PCMs) for solar cooking applications by optimizing fin dimensions to address the low thermal conductivity of PCM during the discharging process. A numerical and experimental analysis was conducted to evaluate the impact of fin length and thickness on solidification time and energy storage capacity, balancing the trade-off between heat transfer improvement and PCM volume reduction. Using ANSYS 16.0 for computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations and response surface methodology (RSM) for design optimization, the study employed solar salt (53 % KNO3, 6 % NaNO3, 41 % NaNO2) with a melting point of 142 °C and latent heat of 110 kJ/kg. Key parameters included fin lengths (70 - 140 mm) and thicknesses (0.8 - 1.5 mm), validated experimentally. Results demonstrated that increasing fin length significantly outperformed thickness enhancement; a fin with 1.5 mm thickness and 140 mm length reduced solidification time by 65.97 % compared to a finless system. RSM optimization identified a fin configuration of 0.8 mm thickness and 140 mm length as optimal, achieving complete solidification in 10.21 hours while releasing 2237.91 kJ of stored energy. These findings highlight the critical role of fin geometry in improving PCM efficiency, enabling effective solar energy storage for extended use, and advancing sustainable alternatives to conventional cooking fuels.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure Linked Equations

ce:section.ce:para: The urgent transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy systems is a crucial step towards global climate action [1,2], with solar energy emerging as a pivotal resource due to its abundance, scalability, and potential [3] to drive economic growth [4] while mitigating environmental risks [5]. This shift is essential not only for reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions but also for enhancing energy security and fostering sustainable development worldwide [6–9].

Solar energy is suitable for both off-grid and grid-connected applications due to its widespread availability across diverse geographic locations. One promising application is solar cooking, which can significantly reduce reliance on fossil fuels and lower electricity costs associated with cooking, addressing a daily necessity while promoting sustainable energy use [10]. However, the intermittent nature of solar radiation poses challenges for applications requiring a consistent energy supply, such as cooking. This necessitates the integration of energy storage solutions. Phase change materials (PCMs), characterized by higher energy density, provide a fascinating thermal energy storage solution by storing latent heat during peak solar hours and releasing it during off-sun periods [11,12]. Yet, their widespread deployment is hindered by their low thermal conductivity (0.1 - 0.8 W/m·K) property [13]. Unlike the metallic type of PCMs, most pure PCMs possess low thermal conductivity [14], which is the main contributing factor to the reduction in heat transfer efficiency and the slow charging and discharging processes [15].

Several heat transfer enhancement techniques have been developed to address the low heat transfer rates in energy storage materials, including the incorporation of fins, PCM encapsulation, using multiple PCMs, and composite PCMs [16–18]. Composite PCMs, which incorporate thermally conductive materials like graphite, nanoparticles, or metal foams into the base PCM, improve thermal conductivity by 13 - 33 % [19–22]. However, the addition of conductive materials may slightly reduce latent heat storage capacity due to dilution effects [23]. Cascaded or multiple PCM systems, where PCMs with different phase change temperatures are used in combination, enable more uniform temperature gradients and improved performance [24]. Microencapsulation is another promising technique that enhances the thermal response of PCMs by enclosing particles within micro- or nano-sized shells, increasing the surface area and preventing phase segregation or leakage [25].

Fins are one of the most commonly adaptable solutions due to their simplicity and scalability. Fins increase the heat transfer area and improve thermal exchange between the PCM and its surroundings. Experimental and numerical studies have demonstrated that finned structures significantly reduce solidification and melting times, though they also reduce the overall PCM volume, potentially lowering total energy storage capacity [26,27]. Fins have been investigated for their geometry, number, and configurations. One of the complex fin geometries studied is a twisted fin, and it has shown significant improvement in triple-tube latent heat storage solidification rate by 12 - 23 % compared with straight fins and finless storages. Similarly, the twisted fins have shown better heat transfer improvement in double-tube heat exchangers with 10 - 40 % improvement in comparison to the straight fins and finless storage system [28]. The other complex fin geometries considered are antenna-shaped, Y-shaped, and hollow fins reduce full solidification time by 36 - 51 % through radiative heat transfer and increased thermal penetration [21,29,30]. For instance, triplex-tube systems with hollow fins and ternary hybrid nanoparticles achieved 23 % higher discharging energy [21]. Snowflake-structured fins with Al₂O₃-GO nanoparticles enhance solidification by 24.1 % [31]. Parameters such as fin spacing, fin length, and fin density (number) also contribute to the solidification rate of PCMs. In a study, an increase in the fin number has shown a 370 % heat transfer improvement [32] but the study never considered the effect on the energy storage capacity as the fin volume fraction increases.

With the advancements in the exploitation of fins, their complex geometries and hybrid systems, such as using fins with nano particles, bring competing objectives in melting/solidification time, heat storage capacity, and manufacturing cost, to name a few. Elmaazouzi et al. [33] conducted parametric optimization to optimize annular fin dimensions (length, number, and thickness) to reduce melting and solidification times by 65.04 % and 58.36 %, respectively. Similarly, Deng et al. [34] found angled fins minimized melting time in double-tube systems, but required complex geometries impractical for compact applications. Recent computational advances, like topology optimization, have revolutionized fin design. Wang et al. [35] achieved a 74 % boost with dendritic fins using only 6 % of fin volume, while Lu et al. [36] reported a 67 % efficiency increase with topology-optimized fins, though high costs limit practical adoption. Cost-effective approaches suggest that high-conductivity fins can reduce containment vessel needs by 30 % [37].

Despite significant advancements, there remain gaps in the research on PCM-based thermal energy storage systems for solar cooking applications. Existing studies focus on industrial-scale applications and neglect the specific challenges in solar cooking, such as the intermittent operations, which are crucial in showing a diversified perspective in the fins-PCM integration. The other gap observed is in the optimization process, where melting and solidification rates are mostly studied, giving less attention to the volume displacement of PCM, an important parameter in performance, space, and cost constraints. Additionally, due to the large number of PCMs with different thermal properties, their performance in various applications is understudied. This study builds upon our previous work [38], but presents a more focused approach on the solidification of PCMs that aims to address the heat transfer drawbacks by designing and optimizing a finned PCM storage system that enhances heat transfer while maintaining high energy storage capacity. Such advancements are expected to contribute to the development of efficient and sustainable solar cooking technologies.

This study aims to parametric study of fins and their optimization during the solidification of a PCM energy storage system using CFD simulations and experimental validations, with the following objectives:
1.
To evaluate the impact of fin geometry (length, thickness) on solidification rates and energy storage capacity using CFD simulations.
2.
To compare the performance of the finned system with conventional finless configurations.
3.
To employ response surface methodology (RSM) for resolving the heat transfer and PCM energy storage capacity.
4.
To experimentally validate the optimized design in solidification for the no-load cooking condition.

Three methods were used to conduct the study: numerical analysis, experimentation, and RSM optimization. To begin with, a PCM material suitable for the cooking temperature range, with desirable physical and thermochemical properties, was selected. The PCM was quantified based on the energy demand of small households in rural Ethiopia. A cylindrical PCM tank was designed to accommodate the fins and the PCM, with the cooking pan placed at the top. Rectangular aluminium fins were chosen due to their cost-effectiveness and availability.

After specifying the design and material selections, a set of fin lengths and thicknesses was prepared for numerical simulation. The numerical simulation assessed the performance of the fin parameters regarding the solidification rate of the PCM. The energy storage capacity was calculated analytically, considering the volume reduction of the PCM due to the presence of fins. For the numerical simulation results' reliability, mesh and time-step independence tests were performed before accepting the results.

The next step involved using the simulation data in Design Expert software for optimization, to determine the optimum fin dimensions that provide the best trade-off between solidification time and heat storage capacity. Finally, an experiment was conducted to validate the numerical results. The optimized fin design was used in the experimental setup.

During the experiment, temperature measurements were taken using thermocouples in both radial (on the top pan) and vertical directions to observe the temperature distribution. The ambient air temperature was also measured during the experiment. Fig. 1 illustrates the methodology workflow.

The solidification process was modelled by using ANSYS Fluent 16. For solidification modeling, FLUENT uses an enthalpy-porosity technique [39]. The enthalpy-porosity method is ideal for modelling phase change, especially during natural convection and transient solidification, as it simplifies the process by avoiding complex interface tracking methods like volume of fluid (VOF). It is robust, efficient, and widely validated in PCM studies [40–42]. In this method, the melt interface is not explicitly tracked. Instead, a parameter called the liquid fraction is assigned to each cell, representing the fraction of the cell volume in liquid form. This fraction is updated iteratively based on an enthalpy balance. The mushy zone, where the liquid fraction ranges between 0 and 1, is treated as a pseudo-porous medium. As the material solidifies, porosity decreases gradually from 1 to 0, effectively reducing velocities to zero once the material fully solidifies.

ANSYS Design Modeler is used to create the geometry of the finned PCM inside the cylindrical container for numerical simulations, as shown in Fig. 2 . The 2D geometry is cut in half (symmetry) to reduce computational resources. The 2D assumption is due to the reason that the dominating heat flow is in the vertical direction and across the width, considering the variable length and thickness with a constant fin depth. The fins vary in thickness at 0.8, 1, and 1.5 mm and in length at 70, 105, and 140 mm.

The geometry created in Design Modeler is discretized using the ANSYS meshing tool. The PCM is treated as the fluid domain, while the fin is considered the solid domain. To ensure better accuracy, a fine grid size is used. The mesh independence test is conducted and the details can be found in the results and discussion section.

The boundary and initial condition assumptions for modelling the solidification are as follows:
•
The Boussinesq approximation is used to model the effects of natural convection.
•
The gravitation forces are considered in the model.
•
The initial temperature of the system is set as constant and uniform at 500 K, which is molten PCM.
•
The top surface of the PCM, which is the 2 mm aluminium pan, is subjected to mixed convection and radiation, with a convective heat transfer coefficient of 25
W / (
m
2
. K )
, and an ambient temperature of 300 K. Other parameters are set to the default setting for radiation.
The energy and momentum equations are solved using the following governing equations.

For the energy equation, the enthalpy of the material is computed as the sum of the sensible enthalpy, h, and the latent heat, ∆H:
(1)
H = h +
Δ
H
Where h is expressed as:
(2)
h =
h
r e f
+
∫
T
r e f
T
c p
d T
href and Tref are the reference enthalpy and temperature, while Cp is the specific heat capacity at a constant pressure.

The liquid fraction, β , is expressed as:
(3)
β =
{
0 ,
i f
T
<
T
s o l i d u s

T

,
i f
T
s o l i d u s
<
T
<
T
l i q u i d u s

The latent heat is expressed with the liquid fraction and, latent heat of the material, L as:
(4)
Δ
H = β L

The energy equation for melting and, solidification problems is condensed to the form as:
(5)
∂
(
ρ H
)
∂ t
+ ∇ .

Where
ρ = d e n s i t y
,
H = e n t h a l p y
,
v →
= f l u i d
v e l o c i t y
and,
S = s o u r c e
t e r m
.
The temperature solution is obtained through an iterative process between the energy Eq. (5) and the liquid fraction Eq. (3).

For the momentum, the enthalpy-porosity technique treats the mushy region (partially solidified region) as a porous medium. The porosity in each cell is set equal to the liquid fraction in that cell. In fully solidified regions, the porosity is equal to zero, which extinguishes the velocities in these regions. The momentum sink due to the reduced porosity in the mushy zone takes the following form:
(6)
S =
(
1 − β
)
2
(
β
3
+ ϵ
)
A
m u s h
(
v →
−
v →
p
)
where ϵ is a small number (0.001) to avoid a division by zero and β is the liquid volume fraction.
A
m u s h
is a constant called the mushy zone constant, which measures the amplitude of the damping. The higher the mushy constant shows the faster the transition of the velocity of the material to zero as it solidifies. Very high values of the mushy constant can cause the solution to oscillate. The
v →
p
is the solid velocity that results from the drawing of solidified material out of the domain. It is also known as the pull velocity [39].

In FLUENT, the solution approach employed includes the second-order upwind scheme for solving the momentum and energy equations, the SIMPLE method for pressure–velocity coupling, and the PRESTO scheme for pressure correction. The relaxation factors were maintained at their default values, set at 0.3 for pressure, 0.7 for momentum, 1 for energy and body forces, 1 for density, and 0.9 for the liquid fraction.

The PCM material, which is commonly called HITEC salt, was prepared from KNO3, NaNO3, and NaNO2 in the mass percentages of 53, 6, and 41, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 . The thermophysical properties of the selected PCM material are shown in Table 1 .

Focusing on the fin dimensions to isolate geometric effects, aluminium was used as the fin material. However, material choice significantly impacts thermal performance. Copper’s higher conductivity reduces solidification time further, but comes at a higher cost.

Fig. 4 shows the different fin thicknesses prepared based on the most locally available aluminium sizes. The fins have thicknesses of 0.8 mm, 1 mm, and 1.5 mm, ranging from the smallest to the largest, with a length of 140 mm.

The PCM was heated on an electric stove until it fully melted. The melting process was controlled to 540 K using the thermocouple reading to ensure complete melting and avoid overheating. After being poured into the storage cylinder, there is a slight temperature drop. Temperature measurements were taken once it reached 500 K to match the initial condition in the numerical simulation and to control the experiment. The storage cylinder consists of two concentric sheets of mild steel, with the space between them filled with ash for insulation. Ash is also used to insulate the bottom by extending the length of the outer cylinder. Ash is chosen for its low cost and accessibility in off-grid communities. While alternative insulators such as fiberglass could be used in real applications, ash mimics practical scenarios in resource-limited settings.
The fins are attached to the circular pan with thermocouples, as shown in Fig. 5 (b) to measure the temperature distribution while discharging at the bottom of the pan.

Design-Expert 11 software was employed to generate the Central Composite Design (CCD). For two input parameters, namely length and thickness, a total of nine design points were required to develop the response surface model. For the analysis, α = 1 was selected as the appropriate value. The factor levels used in the study, as presented in Table 2 , were determined based on this α value. Consequently, the CCD design adopts a face-centred composite configuration with the designated design points. Table 2, outlines the input parameters and corresponding response variables for the intended optimization.

The optimization process considers two response variables: full solidification time and maximum energy storage capacity. The objective is to minimize the solidification time while maximizing the energy storage capacity. To quantitatively analyse the maximum energy storage capacity, it is defined as the total sum of sensible and latent heat across the entire domain at the onset of the solidification process. Therefore, the total thermal energy released by the PCM is evaluated as:
Total heat capacity=Sensible heat+Latent heat
(7)
E
( kJ )
=
m PCM
C p
(
T i
−
T m
)
+
m PCM
h fl

The mass of PCM is calculated as,
m PCM
=
ρ PCM
V PCM
, Mass of the fin is,
m fin
=
ρ Al
V fin
, Volume of PCM,
V PCM
=
V total
−
V fin
, Volume of fin,
V fin
=
thickness * length * width
, Volume of the cylinder,
V total
= π
r
2
H
.
T i
represents the initial temperature of the molten PCM, which is 500 K, while
T m
denotes the melting temperature of the PCM, set at 415 K.

The performance of phase change materials is largely influenced by the fins, which in turn enhance heat transfer and accelerate solidification. This section presents the results and discusses the effect of fin thickness and length on solidification rate, comparing finned PCMs with the base case, which is pure PCM. Optimization is also conducted to minimize solidification time and maximize energy storage capacity. A significant portion of this work is adapted from the author's thesis [38], where a more detailed analysis can be found. The following subsections present key findings on thermal behaviour, parametric influence, and optimization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Grid and time step independence study
ce:section.ce:para: Testing the mesh size and time step size is essential in ensuring CFD simulations. This process confirms that the solution remains stable and insensitive to further spatial or temporal resolution refinements. The liquid fraction was taken as the key parameter to evaluate phase change behavior. Number of elements, ranging from 1680 to 14,645, and time step values in the range 1 to 3 s were tested for the liquid fraction. Fig. 6 shows the variation of the liquid fraction for both cases. During the solidification of the PCM for 2 hrs, the liquid fraction was found nearly constant (0.94) for all elements and time steps, with a maximum variation of <1 %. Therefore, the solution is reliable across all given elements or time steps. For this case, a time step of 1 s with 2436 elements is used to simulate the solidification process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of fin thickness on PCM solidification rate
ce:section.ce:para: The study has shown the solidification rates with the fins included by varying their thickness in the CFD. A storage without fins or pure PCM is used as a benchmark for comparison to study the effect of the fins (Table 3 ), Presents the liquid fraction contours of a PCM in both fin-free and finned configurations with varying thicknesses, highlighting their impact on the solidification rate. In the finless storage, solidification occurs slowly, with the liquid fraction remaining high for a prolonged period. In the beginning, the PCM shows a uniform thin layer of solid, which is exposed to the environment. It is observed that until 8.5 hours, more than half of the total volume remains in the liquid state, prolonging the solidification process. The solidified region acts as insulation, hindering faster heat discharge. As a result, the finless system demonstrates poor thermal performance.

With fins, the solidification process improves significantly. Initially, the presence of the fins doesn’t show a better contribution to the solidification, but later on, when the top part is solidified, the fins are used as a heat pipe, which guides thermal penetration from inside out. At 3 hr, the presence of fins facilitates better heat transfer, causing a more noticeable solid region to form. By 5 hr, the effect of fin thickness becomes clearer, as the 1.5 mm results in faster solidification compared to the 0.8 mm and 1 mm fins. The solidification rates for the 0.8 mm, 1 mm, and 1.5 mm thick fins with all having the same length of 140 mm, were 10.24, 9.75, and 8.9 hours, respectively. It was observed fast heat release of PCM increases as the thickness of the fin increases.

Initially, the PCM is in a molten state, where natural convection plays a significant role. However, as solidification progresses, heat transfer becomes primarily conduction-dominated. The temperature history is shown in Fig. 7 also shows that the PCM with the thicker fins gets its temperature lowered due to fast discharging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of fin length on solidification rate
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of fin length on the solidification rate with the temperature distribution is presented on Table 4 . Long fins increase the interfacial surface area between the PCM and the fin surface, enhancing heat transfer. The temperature gradient along the fins, extending across both solidified and liquid regions of the PCM, further promotes effective thermal exchange. In addition, the presence of long fins helps maintain a more uniform temperature distribution within the PCM.

The results indicate that the addition of fins largely improved the solidification rate of the PCM, with fin length having a more significant effect than fin thickness. From the 9 design points considered, the fin with 1.5 mm thickness and 140 mm length reduced the solidification time by 65.97 % compared to the finless system. This confirms that increasing fin length is an effective strategy for accelerating heat dissipation and improving the efficiency of PCM-based thermal energy storage systems.

A comparative analysis was conducted between the CFD simulation results and the experimental data to assess the model's accuracy in predicting the thermal behaviour during the phase change process. Fig. 8 illustrates temperature evolution of both the CFD and experimental cases over time for the optimum fin design. From the figure, it is evident that both the CFD model and the experimental data show the similar qualitative trend which is a progressive temperature drop due to the solidification of the phase change material. However, a notable time shift is observed; while the experimental system completes solidification in approximately 4 hours, the CFD model predicts a delayed completion, requiring around 7.5 hours. This lag is a common phenomenon in numerical PCM models due to the use of idealized boundary conditions (perfect insulation with no external heat dissipation), constant material properties, and possible simplifications in the heat transfer model. Additionally, the experimental results’ rapid solidification rate during the first hour is attributed to heat loss through the ash insulation and minor gaps in the sealing of the storage system.

To quantify the discrepancy, four statistical metrics were employed over the overlapping time domain of the first 4 hours as shown in Table 5 . The Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) values indicate an average deviation of approximately 33 - 36 K between the CFD and experimental values. The MAPE of 7.80 % falls within an acceptable range for thermal system modelling. The negative R² value suggests that the CFD model underperforms in closely tracking the experimental temperature trend during the early solidification phase. However, this does not imply a lack of predictive capability, but rather emphasizes the temporal mismatch in solidification progression. Such deviations are reported in prior PCM modelling literature [44], where factors like heat loss, contact resistance, and sensor response time are difficult to capture accurately in simulations. In the present study, the CFD model successfully reproduces the overall thermal behaviour and final steady state temperature, confirming its validity for design and optimization purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of fins on pan surface temperature distribution
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 , illustrates the comparison between experimental and CFD results for the temperature distribution of the pan for the no-load condition (without actual cooking). The heat from the pan is lost to the environment due to the combined effect of natural convection and radiation. Temperature measurements were recorded radially, starting from the centre of the pan towards the periphery - at 0 mm, 30 mm, 60 mm, and 90 mm. The results indicate that the temperature is higher near the centre of the pan, where the fins are positioned closer together, compared to the outer edge of the cylindrical container. The limited space near the cylindrical container (housing the PCM capsule) prevents the effective placement of rectangular fins, resulting in a lower temperature in this region.

The graph for the temperature of the pan with time shows a similar pattern to that of the PCM solidification graph, where the temperature during the initial hours is high, and at the phase change of the PCM, the temperature at the pan is maintained to be constant, which later drops for sensible solidification. The temperature vs. time graph for the pan follows a similar pattern to the PCM solidification graph. Initially, the temperature is high, and during the phase change of the PCM, the temperature of the pan remains stable. This is followed by a gradual decrease during the later solidification phase. As shown in Fig. 9, with the sharply increasing curve, the temperature of the pan begins to rise sharply at the start of the solidification process.

The effect of the length of fins on the pan’s surface temperature is also shown in Fig. 10 . The results indicate that the longer fin (140 mm) enhanced heat transfer and, consequently, solidification more effectively than the 70 mm and 105 mm fins, as shown by the graph, which consistently displays higher temperatures for the 140 mm fin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response surface methodology (RSM) for geometric optimization
ce:section.ce:para: The Coefficient of Determination (R²) quantifies the proportion of total variation explained by the model and typically ranges between 0 and 1, with 1 representing the optimal value. High R² values indicate that the predictive model accurately captures the behaviour of the system. In Fig. 11 , the comparison between predicted values and CFD results for solidification time and heat capacity is illustrated, indicating the accuracy of the response surface in replicating the actual CFD outcomes.

For this model, the R² values are 0.997 and 0.9999 for the time and heat capacity analyses, respectively. These values, being exceptionally close to 1, demonstrate a strong correlation between the predicted and observed results. The high R² values can be attributed to the robustness of the response surface methodology (RSM) in modelling complex relationships between multiple variables. RSM effectively captures the nonlinear interactions between geometric parameters like fin length, thickness, and their impact on heat transfer and solidification behaviour. In addition, the precision of the CFD simulations plays a key role in achieving such high R² values. The CFD model is designed to incorporate accurate physical properties, boundary conditions, and heat transfer mechanisms, ensuring a close match with real-world phenomena. The strong alignment between the CFD results and the predicted values not only validates the accuracy of the response surface model but also reinforces the reliability of the optimization process in identifying optimal geometric configurations.

Fig. 12 (a) and (b) present the response surfaces generated during the optimization process. The total solidification time for the nine design points ranges from 8.9 to 15.23 hours, while the total heat capacity varies between 2185.87 and 2267.64 kJ as shown in Table 6 . The observed variation in solidification time and heat capacity can be attributed to the geometric characteristics of the fins, which directly influence the heat transfer rate and energy storage efficiency. Longer and thicker fins (140 mm with 1.5 mm) provide a larger surface area for heat dissipation, which accelerates the solidification process. However, the increased volume fraction of the fin reduces the heat storage capacity. In contrast, the maximum heat capacity is achieved with thinner and shorter fins (70 mm long and 0.8 mm thick) due to the smaller volume fraction of the fin and less thermal penetration to the length of the cylinder. Fig. 12(c) illustrates the desirability response surface, representing the optimal design that balances competing objectives of minimal solidification time with maximum heat capacity.

The optimal fin configuration was determined to have a thickness of 0.8 mm and a length of 140 mm, resulting in a full solidification time of 10.21 hours and a heat storage capacity of 2237.91 kJ, as shown in Fig. 13 . The result from design expert shows that, the dominant heat transfer enhancement parameter was the fin length while thin fins increase the volume fraction of the PCM for maximum energy storage capacity.

The study has explored the effect of fins on improving the solidification rate and optimizing the fin parameters that maximize energy storage and minimize solidification time using RSM optimization. In general, the following points summarize the current work.
•
The fin with dimensions of 1.5 mm thickness and 140 mm length demonstrated a 65.97 % faster solidification rate compared to the system without fins.
•
Increasing the fin length proved to be more effective in enhancing the heat transfer rate than increasing the fin thickness due to higher thermal penetration.
•
RSM optimization identified a 0.8 mm thick, 140 mm long fin as the ideal configuration, achieving full solidification in 10.21 hours while retaining 2237.91 kJ of stored energy.
•
The CFD results demonstrate qualitative agreement with the experimental data regarding the temperature evolution during the solidification process. However, a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 7.8 % suggests a notable statistical deviation.

This study was limited to fin geometry and PCM volume considerations, and further research is recommended to achieve more comprehensive results. Potential directions for future work include
•
Explore material trade-offs, balancing thermal conductivity, cost, and corrosion resistance for solar cooking applications.
•
Extend the investigation for hybrid systems (e.g., fins with nanoparticles), scalability for the community, and cost-benefit analyses.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work, the author(s) used ChatGPT to edit and improve readability. After using this tool/service, the author(s) reviewed and edited the content as needed and take(s) full responsibility for the content of the publication.

Abraha Kahsay Kiros: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Balewgize Amare Zeru: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Debela Geneti Desisa: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Desta Goytom Tewolde: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
